coredata.dc:title: A full-wavelength coverage colorimetric sensor depending on polymer-carbon nanodots from blue to red for visual detection of nitrite via smartphone
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Rui
Ruan, Guotong
Sun, Yujie
Zhao, Duoduo
Yu, Haidong
Zhang, Cheng-Wu
Li, Lin
Liu, Jinhua
coredata.dc:description: A key challenge for preparing colorimetric sensor is to achieve full-wavelength color change under visible light. Herein, a full-wavelength coverage visible light-stimulated colorimetric sensor for specific detection of NO2 − is constructed with polymer-carbon nanodots (PCNDs). The developed sensor is based on the principle that the fluorescence of PCNDs can be excited by visible light and visualized with a black background owning to the front and back light being absorbed. The as-achieved sensor exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity for visualization detection of NO2 − in blue (from blue to orange) and red channel (from red to orange), respectively. Furthermore, a ColorAssist with a smartphone is used to obtain ΔRGB values for quantitative analysis of NO2 − based on color intensity. Importantly, the proposed sensor can be applied for on-site NO2 − detection in meat products with satisfied results. The developed strategy is not only portable and accessible for in situ analysis, but also has dual output color signal and full-wavelength coverage. As far as we know, this is the first full-wavelength coverage visible light-stimulated dual-channel colorimetric sensor for visual detection of NO2 −. Therefore, we believe that these studies will broaden the application of colorimetric sensor in on-site environmental monitoring and diseases diagnostic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer-carbon nanodots
Full-wavelength
Visual detection
Colorimetric sensor
Nitrite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective sensing and reaction kinetics of nitrite in dual-colorimetric sensor
Detection limit for nitrite in dual-colorimetric sensor
Detection limit for nitrite by dual-colorimetric sensor in urine
Preparation of real samples and visual detection of NO2 − in ham sausage
Design principle of colorimetric sensor in visible light
Colorimetric sensor for visual recognition of nitrite ion under visible light
Investigation on the response mechanism for colorimetric sensor toward NO2 − in visible light
Colorimetric sensor visual recognition of nitrite ion in urine and meat products

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics of Hunan University (Grant nos. 2018008 ), and the financial support from the National Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 21675085 , 21505072 , 81672508 , 61505076 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province for Distinguished Young Scholars ( BK20170041 , BK20170042 ) and Key University Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (No. 16KJA180004 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of cross section of a horizontal integrated visual sensor based on PCNDs without (A) and with (B) black background under visible light illuminating.

The absorption and fluorescence spectrum (A) of PCNDs without and with NO2 −, (B) The EEM of the PCNDs, (C) The different color background photographs of PCNDs without and with NO2 −, (D) TEM, (E) AFM and (F) XPS of the PCNDs.

(A) Synthesis of PCNDs and its sensing performance for the detection of NO2 − in colorimetric and fluorescent manner by visible light illuminating; The ΔRGB values for colorimetric (B) and fluorescent (C) of PCNDs versus the concentration of NO2 − from 0 to 160 μM in Tris-HCl buffer (20 mM, pH = 7.0). Inset: a linear relationship of the ΔRGB values versus the concentration of NO2 −.The ΔRGB values of colorimetric (D) and fluorescent (E) detection of PCNDs (0.4 mg/mL) versus different anion ions: 1, none; 2, P2O7 4−; 3, H2PO4 −; 4, HPO4 2−; 5, PO4 3−; 6, CO3 2−; 7, HCO3 −; 8, SO3 2−; 9, SO4 2−; 10,SCN−; 11, F−; 12,Cl−; 13, Br−; 14, I−; 15, S2−; 16, Ac−; 17, NO3 −; 18, NO2 −; All the concentrations of anion ions are 400 μM. Inset: The colorimetric and fluorescent photographs of PCNDs with different anion ions are under visible light illumination.

Fluorescence emission spectra (A) and UV–vis spectra (B) of PCNDs in the presence of different concentrations of NO2 −; (C) Time-dependent relative absorbance intensity of PCNDs at 385 nm and 610 nm upon the addition of NO2 − (50 μM); (D) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity of the PCNDs upon the addition of NO2 − (50 μM) (λex = 580 nm); (E) Fluorescence decay profiles of PCNDs with and without NO2 −;(F) Zeta potential: (a) PCNDs, (b) PCNDs + NO2 −.

Colorimetric and fluorescence photographs of PCNDs without and with black background toward different concentration of NO2 − in Tris-HCl buffer (20 mM, pH = 7.0) with 5% urine sample under visible light illuminating; The calibration plots of the ΔRGB values of colorimetric (A) and fluorescence (B) responses of PCNDs with different concentrations of NO2 −. Inset: a linear relationship of the ΔRGB values versus the concentration of NO2 −. (C) Colorimetric (left) and fluorescent (right) photographs of detection of NO2 − at spiked ham sausage. (D) Comparison of the quantitative detection results among colorimetry and fluorescence. Error bars represent the mean value of three measurements at the same ham sausage and the corresponding standard deviation.

head.ce:title: A full-wavelength coverage colorimetric sensor depending on polymer-carbon nanodots from blue to red for visual detection of nitrite via smartphone

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Flexible Electronics (KLOFE) and Institute of Advanced, Materials (IAM), Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced, Materials (SICAM), Nanjing Tech University (NanjingTech), 30 South Puzhu Road, Nanjing, 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Flexible Electronics (KLOFE) and Institute of Advanced
Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Flexible Electronics (KLOFE) and Institute of Advanced, Materials (IAM), Jiangsu National Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced, Materials (SICAM), Nanjing Tech University (NanjingTech), 30 South Puzhu Road, Nanjing, 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A key challenge for preparing colorimetric sensor is to achieve full-wavelength color change under visible light. Herein, a full-wavelength coverage visible light-stimulated colorimetric sensor for specific detection of NO2 − is constructed with polymer-carbon nanodots (PCNDs). The developed sensor is based on the principle that the fluorescence of PCNDs can be excited by visible light and visualized with a black background owning to the front and back light being absorbed. The as-achieved sensor exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity for visualization detection of NO2 − in blue (from blue to orange) and red channel (from red to orange), respectively. Furthermore, a ColorAssist with a smartphone is used to obtain ΔRGB values for quantitative analysis of NO2 − based on color intensity. Importantly, the proposed sensor can be applied for on-site NO2 − detection in meat products with satisfied results. The developed strategy is not only portable and accessible for in situ analysis, but also has dual output color signal and full-wavelength coverage. As far as we know, this is the first full-wavelength coverage visible light-stimulated dual-channel colorimetric sensor for visual detection of NO2 −. Therefore, we believe that these studies will broaden the application of colorimetric sensor in on-site environmental monitoring and diseases diagnostic.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite is widely applied in the food processing industry as food preservative and dyeing agent [1,2]. Excessive accumulation of nitrite in the body can cause great deleterious effects on health, especially for infants and children [3,4]. Nitrite can interact with amines, amides or proteins to form highly carcinogenic N-nitrosamines compounds, which cause a series of medical problems such as cancer, hypertension, methemoglobinemia, and neurodegenerative diseases [5,6]. International Agency for Research on Cancer of the World Health Organization (WHO) has enrolled nitrite in 2A carcinogens list. The maximum allowable content of NO2 − in drinking water regulated by WHO is 3.0 mg/L, and that of the United States Environmental Protection Agency is 1.0 mg/L [7,8]. Therefore, it is of great significance and necessity to establish reliable and accurate methods for nitrite detection in human body and the environmental system.

To date, many methods for determination of nitrite have been developed, such as colorimetric [9], fluorescence [10], chemiluminescence [11], electrophoresis [12], chromatography [13], mass spectrum [14,15]. Among them, colorimetric strategies have attracted widely attention due to features of response-fast, easy to operate, visual and convenient. Nevertheless, most of visual methods for nitrite detection focused on only one channel (colorimetric or fluorescence) strategy. For example, Ramaiah groups reported a colorimetric probe for sensitive detection of nitrite in water via a visual color change from blue to green. Guo et al. exploited a ratiometric fluorescent-based portable agarose hydrogel test kit for on-spot visual detection of nitrite using carbon dots [16]. There were a few dual-channels visual (colorimetric and fluorescence) approaches for detection of nitrite reported. For example, Hou group presented a dual-readout sensor of fluorescent and colorimetric for sensitive detection of nitrite based on Griess assay [17]. To achieve the visual fluorescence detection of nitrite, excitation with a specific wavelength of light is needed, which limits the application of this method. Hence, the dual-channel visual methods in an open environment for the detection of nitrite under visible light remain huge challenge.

The RGB model, which can reproduce a broad array of colors by mixing three primary components according to red, green and blue in different ratio, is frequently employed to represent the colors by color coordinates for sensing, representation and display of images in electronic systems [18–21]. Recently, the RGB model has been successfully applied in many visual sensors to quantitatively analyze the color changes of a colorimetric picture illuminated by visible light or fluorescent picture illuminated by ultraviolet radiation [22–24]. The ubiquity of the RGB color model, particularly in digital cameras and smartphones, has provided such an opportunity to exploit their application for complex analytical tasks in a convenient way [25–28].

Herein, a full-wavelength coverage visible light-stimulated dual-channel colorimetric sensor is constructed for sensitive detection of nitrite depending on polymer-carbon nanodots (PCNDs). The synthesized PCNDs show blue in visible light and red fluorescence illuminated by 365 nm UV light. With black background, the fluorescence of PCNDs can be visualized under visible light. The developed dual-channel colorimetric sensor exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity in visual detection of NO2 − in blue (from blue to orange) and red channel (from red to orange), respectively. Additionally, a ColorAssist software installed on smartphone is successfully applied for dual-channel visual detection of NO2 − in meat products. Therefore, the developed approach not only has dual output color signal and full-wavelength coverage, but also is portable and accessible for on-site detection without using complex instruments.

Dopamine hydrochloride (DA) was purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). O-phenylenediamine was purchased from Macklin Corp. (Shanghai, China). Sodium nitrite (NaNO2, 99%) was obtained from Aladdin-Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was ordered from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Na4P2O7•10H2O, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, Na3PO4, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, Na2SO3, Na2SO4, NaSCN, NaF, NaCl, NaBr, NaI, Na2S, NaAc and NaNO3 were purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tris-HCl buffer (20 mM, pH 7.0) was prepared using metal free reagents in distilled water. Urine samples were collected from the three healthy adult volunteers and immediately frozen and stored at 4 °C until assayed. Other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water with a conductivity of 18 MΩ cm−1 was used in all experiments. Filter paper made from pure cellulose was from Whatman PLC (Whatman, UK).

A UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-1750, SHIMADZU, Japan) was used for the UV–Vis absorption spectra analyses. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer and a xenon lamp as the excitation source. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were acquired by using a JEM-2010 (UHR, JEOE, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained on a JEM-2010 (UHR, JEOE, Japan) using an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a PHI Quantum 5000 XPS system (Physical Electronics, USA). Whereas XPS was used to characterize the elemental composition and bonding configuration. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a VECTOR 22 spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany). The luminescence decay curves were measured with a LP920 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Raman measurements were done at room temperature using an Alpha300 M + confocal dispersive Raman spectrometer equipped with a macro-Raman measurement accessory (WITEC). All pH measurements were obtained using a pHS-25 pH meter (Shanghai, China). The Image J is scriptable Java app for scientific image processing, and the used version of Image J software is 2006.02.01. The “colorAssist” was a convenient tool for mobile phone software supplied by FTLapps, lnc.,[29], which can be downloaded from app store and only suitable for macintosh system, and the “color Recognizer” was a convenient and quick color picking app for android system.

The PCNDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization [30]. Briefly, dopamine hydrochloride (DA) (90 mg) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) (10 mg) were mixed with water (10 mL) using HCl to adjust the pH, after then the mixture solution was transferred to an autoclave (20 mL) and reacted for 8 h at 200 °C. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the dark blue product in the autoclave was collected for subsequent experiments.

Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) stipulates that the wavelength of standard red primary color is 700 nm, the standard green primary color is 546.1 nm and the standard blue primary color is 435.8 nm. When equal amounts of red, green and blue tricolor light are mixed, white light can be produced. At this time, the units of red, green and blue are called basic or basic color units, and are marked with symbols (R), (G), (B). The CIE standard observer functions were experimentally determined in 1931 to use as an approximation of the spectral response of the eye. These three primary-color-matching functions had allowed the definition of color in terms of three tristimulus values [X Y Z]. These visible spectra were imported into the spreadsheet and were fitted with the color-matching functions using eq (1) [31].

The Delta RGB (ΔRGB) calculation (eq (1)) define the magnitude of the vector between the control (original) RGB value (R0, G0, B0) and the RGB value of measured (Rn, Gn, Bn).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective sensing and reaction kinetics of nitrite in dual-colorimetric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of the assay to NO2 −, a series of anion ions, including P2O7 4−, H2PO4 −, H2PO4 −, PO4 3−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, SCN−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, S2−, Ac−, NO3 −, NO2 − (the concentration of anion ions was 400 μM) were added into the PCNDs (0.4 mg/mL) solutions, respectively. After incubation for 30 min, all photos were collected by using an iPhone camera under visible light illumination. The absorbance and fluorescence spectra of PCNDs before and after adding different anion ions were recorded. Meanwhile, competition tests were also investigated by adding anion ions into the assay with NO2 −. Similarly, reaction kinetics of PCNDs with NO2 − were performed. The absorption of the PCNDs at 380 nm and 610 nm were recorded by UV–vis spectrometry. In order to determine the excitation wavelength of the measured fluorescence spectrum, the emission spectra of the PCND at different excitation wavelengths were recorded. Then the fluorescent intensity of the PCNDs was recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 560 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection limit for nitrite in dual-colorimetric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Absorption and fluorescence experiments of the PCNDs were performed towards various concentrations of NaNO2 in buffer solution (20 mM, pH 7.0), respectively. After incubation for 5 min, the mixtures were detected using colorimetric and fluorescent channel, respectively. For colorimetric detection, the obtained mixtures solution was measured by an UV–vis spectrophotometer and photographed with an iPhone camera under visible light illumination. A calibration curve was obtained depending on the relationship between the concentrations of NO2 − and their corresponding absorbance ratio (A380/A610). For fluorescent detection, the fluorescent spectra of the mixtures were recorded directly by fluorescence spectrophotometer excited at 580 nm and photographed with an iPhone camera under visible light illumination. The calibration curve for NO2 − assay was based on the final concentrations of the NO2 − and signal-to-noise ratio (S/B).

The obtained fluorescence photos and colorimetric photos were analyzed by ImageJ software[32–34]. By confirming and identifying the test spot area without the transition edge of color, the analysis function of ImageJ could calculate the value of each test spot and directly output the average value of RGB. Then these average RGB values were put into the above formula to get ΔRGB values. Thus, the correlation between color and the concentration of NO2 − in the photos could be converted into a regular linear relationship.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection limit for nitrite by dual-colorimetric sensor in urine
ce:section.ce:para: Urine from consent volunteers was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was collected for the following experiments. Absorption and fluorescence experiments of the PCNDs were performed towards various concentration of NaNO2 in diluted urine (5%) solution. Images were collected with an iPhone camera under visible light illumination. According to the above method, the linear equation between NO2 − concentration and ΔRGB was obtained through ImageJ processing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of real samples and visual detection of NO2 − in ham sausage
ce:section.ce:para: The ham sausage was cut into 0.2 cm disks and different concentrations of NO2 − were added to these disks, and then dried in natural environment. The treated disks were soaked in 3 mL Tris-HCl buffer solution for 30 min to get the real sample solutions. The sample solutions with NO2 − concentration of 0 and 20 μM were labeled as qualified sample and unqualified sample, respectively. For detection of NO2 −, the PCNDs solution was directly dropped in these real sample solutions. After 10 min, all photographs of color and fluorescence of solutions were recorded with an iPhone camera. Additionally, the obtained photographs of solutions were further processed by ColorAssist and ImageJ to get two sets of RGB and density-related calibration plots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design principle of colorimetric sensor in visible light
ce:section.ce:para: In present study, the PCNDs is synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization of the dopamine hydrochloride (DA) and o-phenylenediamine precursors according to method previously reported by Yang group [30]. The obtained PCNDs show obvious blue in aqueous solution under visible light and emit a strong red fluorescence after excited by 365 nm UV light. Interestingly, we find that by setting a black background, the PCNDs can also show its red fluorescence under visible light. As shown in Fig. S1A, the PCNDs solution is blue and red with and without a black background, respectively, indicating that the black background can effectively make the red fluorescence of PCNDs visible by visible light. We believe that several conditions must be met to enable fluorescence to be visualized under visible light: (1) the samples have high fluorescence quantum yield; (2) the range of the samples excited wavelength should be in the visible range from 320 to 780 nm; (3) the color of the sample can be distinguished from its fluorescence. The obtained PCNDs is satisfied with all the above requirements. These results further indicate that the visual color of the PCNDs can be tunable according to its background.

To reveal the reason for the fluorescence visualization of PCNDs depending on the background under visible light, we explore the whole process of light change with and without background. When there is no background, the light on the opposite side of the sample bottle is absorbed and displays blue light, because the absorbance wavelength of PCNDs ranges from 350 to 650 nm. While in the presence of a background, the color of the visualization will produce different changes depending on the background. With white paper, the light coming from the opposite side of bottle is blocked, and the blue color of the sample is displayed by reflected light of white paper (Fig. 1 A). With black paper, the light on the back side is blocked and absorbed, and the light on the front side is all absorbed (Fig. 1B). Thus, the visualization color is the red fluorescence which is excited from PCNDs by the front light. Fig. 2 A shows the color and absorbance spectrum of PCNDs with and without NO2 −, respectively. In the absence of NO2 −, the color of PCNDs turn blue to orange and the corresponding absorbance spectrum decrease at 600 nm and increase at 360 nm in the presence of NO2 −, indicating that the NO2 − can cause the color change of PCNDs. Similarly, NO2 − can render the color change of PCNDs from red to orange with the black background in visible light (Fig. S1). The corresponding fluorescence spectra of PCNDs at 600 nm decrease significantly in the presence of NO2 −. These results suggest that the developed visible light-excited colorimetric sensor can be used to detect NO2 − in a wide range of color from blue to red.

To further clarify the morphological structure, functional groups and optical properties of obtained PCNDs, characterization analyses are performed. Firstly, three-dimension excitation-emission matrix (EEM) fluorescence spectroscopy is used to characterize the optical properties of PCNDs. The EEM results show one main peak at 625 nm after excited by 560 nm along with an excitation independent fluorescence behavior (Fig. 2B), and the fluorescence emission spectra of PCNDs by different excitation wavelengths also could characterize excitation independent fluorescence behavior (Fig. S2A). In addition, the different color background can affect the visualization color of PCNDs solution without and with NO2 − (Fig. 2C). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) confirm the morphologies of the obtained PCNDs (Fig. 2D). And the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image has been calculated that the atomic lattice is approximately 0.29 nm, which is typical atomic lattice of carbon atoms (Fig. 2D). The image displays that PCNDs have a relative narrow size distribution of 2–4 nm (Fig. S2B). AFM analysis shows that the height of PCNDs is about 3.6 nm (Fig. 2E). The results suggest that the morphological of obtained PCNDs is approximately spherical. While the XPS spectra indicate the element composition and valence state of PCNDs. Four major elements are identified from wide scan XPS spectra (Fig. 2F). The peaks at 532, 400, 284 and 198 eV represent the characteristic peaks of O 1s, N 1s, C 1s and Cl 2p, respectively. Table S1 lists the contents and ratios of surface groups in PCNDs according to the XPS results (Fig. S3). Additionally, the Raman spectrum is further adopted to characterize the structure of PCNDs. The two peaks of 1337 cm−1 and 1556 cm−1 correspond to the D and G band respectively present, indicating the formation of the mixture of amorphous carbon and nanocrystalline graphite (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric sensor for visual recognition of nitrite ion under visible light
ce:section.ce:para: The application of a colorimetric sensor for sample analysis is attractive owning to visibility, simple to perform, stable, and inexpensive. However, quantification of the visible signal is challenging. To obtain accurate quantitative data, we utilized an image software to measure the red, green and blue (RGB) signal values of the color products. The analyte concentration is determined based on the photo taken with a smartphone, then the pictures are processed with photoshop to read and correlate the color intensities of the color products with the analyte concentrations according to the RGB values. The digital image is analyzed by measuring the color intensity of the generated color in each respective detection region. Based on the color change of obtained PCNDs and its sensing performance, we can use RGB model for accurate quantitative the concentration of NO2 − (Fig. 3 A).

To determine the sensitivity of our assay toward NO2 −, the different concentrations of NO2 − are added to the PCNDs-based colorimetric sensor, and the color changes of the PCNDs are analyzed using the ImageJ. Without or with the white background, the photograph of PCNDs in response to NO2 − are obtained. The color of PCNDs gradually changes from blue to orange as NO2 − level increasing. As presented in Fig. 3B, the ΔRGB value increases as NO2 − concentration increases. It clearly exhibits a good linear relationship between the ΔRGB values and NO2 − concentrations in the range from 0 to 130 μM, and the regression equation is expressed as y = 11.3196 + 1.2697× (R2 = 0.9685) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 10 μM. While using the black background, the PCNDs solution shows a red color under visible light illumination. The red color is derived from PCNDs, which is consist with its fluorescence spectra. The visualization of red fluorescence of PCNDs owes to its excitation wavelength in the range of visible wavelength. Thus, when setting a black background and absorbing all the transmission light, the red fluorescence of PCNDs displays. The visible fluorescence titration of the NO2 − is carried out in PCNDs solution. The visible red fluorescence intensity of PCNDs is assessed using ΔRGB values. Upon the addition of increasing concentrations of NO2 −, the visual sensor shows a good linear relationship (y = 1.3734 + 0.5578×, R2 = 0.9873) within the range of 0–80 μM with a limitation of detection (LOD) of 10 μM (Fig. 3C). These results demonstrate that the sensor produce the double signal response to NO2 −. Based on the change of the background, the detection of NO2 − could be visualized by fluorescence and colorimetric with naked eye under visible light. To evaluate the selectivity of our assay, various anions in terms of P2O7 4−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, PO4 3−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, SCN−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, S2−, Ac−, NO3 −, and NO2 − in the system are further investigated. It is clearly shown in Fig. 3D and E, the significant ΔRGB change in fluorescence and color happen only after addition of NO2 − ions, but not other type of anions. The reason for the color changes is attributed to the reaction of NO2 − with PCNDs which subsequently suppressed the fluorescence of the PCNDs. The results suggest that the colorimetric sensor has excellent selectivity for NO2 − in ΔRGB of fluorescent and colorimetric. In addition, the pictures obtained by other mobile phones are also analyzed through the ImageJ (Table S2 and Fig. S5), which can also realize the quantitative detection of NO2 −. Additionally, the efficacy of NO2 − detection with PCNDs is further confirmed by Uv–vis and fluorescent determination (Fig. 4 A and Fig. 4B). As shown in Fig. S6, the LOD of our fluorescent and absorbance method based on the 3σ rule is calculated to be 0.5 μM and 0.3 μM, respectively, which is lower than previously reported ones (Table S3) [35–43]. The real-time records indicate that the absorption and fluorescence of PCNDs toward NO2 − can reach steady state within about 6 min (Fig. 4C and D). Moreover, the corresponding fluorescence and absorbance spectra of sensor also exhibit good selectivity toward NO2 − (Fig. S7). These results indicate that as developed sensor can achieve rapid and sensitive visual recognition of NO2 − by fluorescence and colorimetry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on the response mechanism for colorimetric sensor toward NO2 − in visible light
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching of the PCNDs by NO2 − ion could be explained with a typical Stern-Volmer equation (I0/I = 1+Ksv [NO2 −]), where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of the PCNDs with and without NO2 −, respectively, and Ksv is the static Stern-Volmer constant. According to the analysis of Stern-Volmer plot, it shows a linear trend with a Ksv of 7.2103 M-1. Additionally, the average fluorescence lifetime of the PCNDs changed from 1.635 to 1.613 ns after adding NO2 −, (Fig. 4E). Comprehensive consideration of the relative large magnitude of Ksv and nearly constant fluorescence lifetime, we think that the fluorescence quenching induced by NO2 − is the static quenching, which results from a stable nonfluorescence complex between surface functional groups of PCNDs and NO2 − [44–46]. There are amino group, hydroxyl and carbonyl on the surface of PCNDs, of which amino group is great majority due to the zeta potential (+10.68 mV) of the PCNDs in H2O. After adding NO2 −, the zeta potential change from +10.68 mV to +0.59 mV (Fig. 4F). We speculate that the NO2 − react with the amino group and result in the decrease of the positive charge of PCNDs. If most NO2 − is reacted with the hydroxyl or carbonyl, the zeta potential value should become more positive. FT-IR characterization is carried out to further support the speculation (Fig. S8). Characterization of the surface functional groups of the PCNDs is further carried out by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum. The stretching vibrations for N–H (3220 and 3170 cm−1), C=C (1554 cm−1), C=O/C=N (1645 cm−1), and C–N (1436 cm−1) bonds indicate the formation of polyaromatic structures and the presence of many nitrogen-containing groups. Compared to the FT-IR spectra of PCNDs without NO2 −, an obvious change at 2260.4 cm−1 is observed with presence of NO2 −, which is attributed to the double bond accumulation stretching vibration peak. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of PCNDs shows an obvious enhancement peak at 370 nm after adding NO2 −, indicating the formation of containing the structure of -N=N- [47] These results confirm the generation of diazonium groups owning to the diazonium reaction between PCNDs and NO2 −. As an electron-withdrawing group, the diazo group makes the electron cloud of the n-electron lone pair not coplanar with the π-electron cloud of the benzene ring, resulting in a forbidden process of the transition from n to π*. Therefore, the large intersystem crossing transition between S1 and T1 leads to the fluorescence quenching of PCNDs [48].

In order to further prove the feasibility of our sensor illuminated by other visible light, the iPhone light is used to illuminate the samples. Fig. S9 presents the photographs of PCNDs solution illuminated by the visible light and iPhone light, respectively. Under visible light, the PCNDs solution shows red (Fig. S9a) and blue (Fig. S9b) in the presence and absence of black background, respectively. While under iPhone light illumination the PCNDs also shows red (Fig. S9c). The results indicate that iPhone light can be used as an excited light and make the PCNDs to emit red fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric sensor visual recognition of nitrite ion in urine and meat products
ce:section.ce:para: To further demonstrate application potency of our method, the colorimetric sensors is used for determination of NO2 − in human urine. The urine is diluted 100 times before analysis. The different concentrations of NO2 − are then added into the PCNDs solution with 5% urine. The ΔRGB values of PCNDs solution without background and with black background are recorded. As shown in Fig. 5 , the addition of urine causes a slight color change of PCNDs solution from blue to orange and from red to orange with and without black background under visible light illumination, respectively, which enable the multi-colored visualization of the NO2 − with naked eye. The calibration curve for determining NO2 − in the human urine sample is obtained by plotting the values of (ΔRGB-Ab) or (ΔRGB-FI) versus the concentrations of NO2 − (Fig. 5A and B), with linear equation of y = 4.1703 + 1.2158× (R2 = 0.9908) and y = 3.3654 + 1.4274× (R2 = 0.9874) in colorimetric and fluorescent channel, and the NO2 − concentration ranging from 0 to 120 μM and from 0 to 80 μM are calculated with LOD of 10 μM. These results suggest that this assay enable the on-site visual detection of NO2 − directly with the naked eyes according to the color changes by visible light illumination and no UV lamp is needed.

To further demonstrate the feasibility of colorimetric sensors for detection of NO2 −, the fluorescent and colorimetric responses of PCNDs towards NO2 − are tested in food products, we use ham sausage as a test model of meat product for NO2 − detection. The ham sausage is purchased from the supermarket and cut the ham sausage into 0.5 cm discs. As shown in Fig. S10, the ham sausage with different concentrations of NO2 − cause an obvious color change of PCNDs solution with and without black background under visible light illumination, respectively, and the visual results can be effectively quantified by ΔRGB which was obtained by ColorAssist. Because ColorAssist is a mobile phone application that can realize simple real-time on-site analysis. Based on the obtained results with calibration curves, the corresponding concentrations of NO2 − in qualified ham sausage in colorimetric and fluorescence channels are 0.041 mg/kg and 0.050 mg/kg, respectively. The unqualified ham sausages are detected to be 81.6 mg/kg and 76.5 mg/kg, respectively (Fig. 5C and D). The detection results of colorimetry and fluorescence image analysis are in good agreement, and the NO2 − in ham sausage can be analyzed qualitatively and quantitatively at the same time in this colorimetric sensor.

In summary, a full-wavelength coverage visible light-stimulated dual-channel colorimetric sensor for highly sensitive and selective determination of NO2 − is constructed based on PCNDs. The principle for developing colorimetric sensor is that the fluorescence of PCNDs can be excited by visible light and visualized under visible light with a black background. The as-achieved sensor exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity for visual detection of NO2 − in blue (from blue to orange) and red channel (from red to orange), respectively. The excellent performance of the sensor can be attributed to the following reasons: (1) the colorimetric sensor has a full-wavelength coverage from blue to red (350–750 nm); (2) the red fluorescence of PCNDs can be visualized under visible light by setting a black background; (3) the sensor can realize the dual-modal signal readout from blue to orange and from red to orange for visualization detection of NO2 − in visible light, and no ultraviolet light is needed to obtain fluorescence; (4) the sensor can achieve quantitative analysis of NO2 − based on color intensity with a ColorAssist to obtain RGB values. Consequently, the developed strategy is not only portable and accessible for in situ analysis, but also has dual output color signal and full-wavelength coverage. The full-wavelength coverage colorimetric sensor will broaden the application of colorimetric sensor in on-site environmental monitoring and diseases diagnostic.
RW, GR, YS and DZ contributed to perform the experiments and data analysis. HY, CWZ and LL revised the manuscript, JHL designed and wrote the manuscript.

Rui Wang: Started the work, synthesis, properties studies and initial manuscript writing. Guotong Ruan: Properties studies and initial manuscript writing. Yujie Sun: Properties studies and test. Duoduo Zhao: Started the work, properties studies and test. Haidong Yu: Revised the manuscript. Cheng-Wu Zhang: Revised the manuscript, and gave some suggestions. Lin Li: Revised the manuscript, and gave some suggestions. Jinhua Liu: Overall supervise and writing the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Opening Project of State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics of Hunan University (Grant nos. 2018008), and the financial support from the National Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 21675085, 21505072, 81672508, 61505076), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province for Distinguished Young Scholars (BK20170041, BK20170042) and Key University Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (No. 16KJA180004).
